coredata.dc:title: A nanozyme-based colorimetric sensor array as electronic tongue for thiols discrimination and disease identification
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xueying
Li, Tian
Hai, Xin
Bi, Sai
coredata.dc:description: Thiol analysis is of vital significance due to the essential roles in disease diagnosis, while the highly similar structures of thiols are a major challenge in practical determination. Herein, a nanozyme-based colorimetric sensor array has been proposed as electronic tongue for excellent discrimination and sensitive quantitation of thiols. The sensing units are fabricated by integrating the terephthalic acid modified graphene quantum dots (TPA@GQDs) with three transition metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) via coordination, respectively, which not only provide sufficient substrate binding sites but also form the metal ion-regulated catalytic active centers. In this way, disparate promotion degrees on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity have been achieved in different metal ion-TPA@GQD ensembles. Based on the strong binding affinity between metal ions and thiols, the catalytic active centers are removed from TPA@GQDs, which inhibits the catalytic activity of sensing unit to diverse degrees. Accordingly, using 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as chromogenic substrate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), each sensing unit can generate differential colorimetric signals (fingerprints) for six thiol analytes, which can be accurately discriminated through linear discriminant analysis (LDA) with a detection limit of 50 nM. In addition, the discrimination of the same thiol with different concentrations and thiol mixtures have also been achieved. Furthermore, inspired by the distinct levels of thiols in practical samples, the proposed sensor array enables the identification of thiol-associated diseases by means of machine learning algorithm, which makes a positive contribution to medical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Nanozyme
Graphene quantum dots
Thiols discrimination
Colorimetry

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of kinetics data of metal ion-TPA@GQDs
Mechanism study of the proposed sensor array

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of TPA@GQD nanozyme. (a) Schematics for the synthesis of TPA@GQDs and the catalytic oxidation of TMB. (b) High-resolution C1s XPS analysis and (c) Zeta potential of TPA@GQDs. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra and (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs and TPA@GQDs, respectively. The excitation wavelength in (d) is 430 nm. (f) Time-dependent absorbance of TMB-H2O2 system at 650 nm and (g) the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra after 120 min reaction under the catalysis of TPA, GQDs and TPA@GQDs (all at 0.25 mg mL−1), respectively. Inset in (f): colorimetric photos of TMB-H2O2 system catalyzed by (1) TPA, (2) GQDs and (3) TPA@GQDs for 120 min. The concentrations of TMB and H2O2 are 0.5 mM and 2.5 mM, respectively.

Mechanism study of the metal ion-TPA@GQD nanozyme-based sensor array for the discrimination of thiols. (a) Schematics for the construction of metal ion-TPA@GQD sensing units and the inhibition of catalytic activity by thiols. (b) TEM images, (c) FT-IR, and (d) S2p and Cu2p XPS analysis of TPA@GQDs and Cu2+-TPA@GQDs in the absence and presence of Cys. (e) UV–vis absorbance values of TMB-H2O2 at 650 nm catalyzed by TPA@GQDs, different metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) and different metal ion-TPA@GQDs in the absence and presence of Cys. The concentrations of TMB, H2O2, metal ions and Cys are 0.5 mM, 2.5 mM, 500 μM and 300 μM, respectively. Scale bar in Fig. 2b: 20 nm.

Performance of the proposed nanozyme-based sensor array for the classification of different kinds of thiols. (a) Colorimetric response patterns [(A-A0)/A0] of this sensor arrays toward six kinds of thiols (300 μM). Error bars represent the standard deviation of six independent measurements. (b) Heat map originated from the absorbance changes [(A-A0)/A0] of the sensor array against six kinds of thiols (300 μM). (c to h) 2D canonical score plots for the first two factors of colorimetric response patterns [(A-A0)/A0] towards 300 μM, 100 μM, 1 μM, 100 nM, 50 nM and 25 nM, respectively.

Performance of the nanozyme-based sensor array for the quantification of one kind of thiol and discrimination of thiols mixture. (a) Heat map and (b) 2D canonical score plots of the sensor array in response to Cys with different concentrations. (c) Linear relationship between Cys concentrations (1 μM–300 μM) and factor 1. 2D canonical score plots of the sensor array in response to (d and e) binary and (f) ternary thiols mixtures with different molar ratios and a total concentration of 100 μM.

Practical applications of the nanozyme-based sensor array. (a) Schematics of diseases identification by discriminating various types of cells and clinical human serums. (b) 2D canonical score plots of the sensor array toward various cells with different concentration of GSH. (c) Plot of the discriminant factor 1, and (d) HCA dendrogram of normal L02 and cancerous HeLa and MCF-7 cells, respectively. (e) ROC curve of the sensor array in discrimination of cancer versus health. (f) 2D canonical score plots of the sensor array toward clinical serum samples with various concentrations of Hcy. (g) Plot of the discriminant factor 1 and (h) HCA dendrogram of serums from patients with cardiovascular disease and healthy volunteers. (i) ROC curve of the sensor array in discrimination of cardiovascular disease versus health. ****p < 0.0001 (Student's t-test).
Schematics of colorimetric sensor array based on metal ion-integrated TPA@GQD nanozyme for thiols discrimination.

head.ce:title: A nanozyme-based colorimetric sensor array as electronic tongue for thiols discrimination and disease identification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiol analysis is of vital significance due to the essential roles in disease diagnosis, while the highly similar structures of thiols are a major challenge in practical determination. Herein, a nanozyme-based colorimetric sensor array has been proposed as electronic tongue for excellent discrimination and sensitive quantitation of thiols. The sensing units are fabricated by integrating the terephthalic acid modified graphene quantum dots (TPA@GQDs) with three transition metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) via coordination, respectively, which not only provide sufficient substrate binding sites but also form the metal ion-regulated catalytic active centers. In this way, disparate promotion degrees on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity have been achieved in different metal ion-TPA@GQD ensembles. Based on the strong binding affinity between metal ions and thiols, the catalytic active centers are removed from TPA@GQDs, which inhibits the catalytic activity of sensing unit to diverse degrees. Accordingly, using 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as chromogenic substrate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), each sensing unit can generate differential colorimetric signals (fingerprints) for six thiol analytes, which can be accurately discriminated through linear discriminant analysis (LDA) with a detection limit of 50 nM. In addition, the discrimination of the same thiol with different concentrations and thiol mixtures have also been achieved. Furthermore, inspired by the distinct levels of thiols in practical samples, the proposed sensor array enables the identification of thiol-associated diseases by means of machine learning algorithm, which makes a positive contribution to medical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols are involved in regulating many physiological functions of organisms (Chen et al., 2016). It has been reported that the abnormal levels of thiols may give rise to a series of diseases (Lin et al., 2018). For example, high level of glutathione (GSH) in human body is associated with many diseases like Alzheimer's disease, acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) and even cancers (Lei et al., 2016; Meng et al., 2020). The increased content of homocysteine (Hcy) will dramatically raise the risk of cardiovascular diseases, such as atherosclerosis, coronary heart disease and thromboembolism (Azad et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2019). In addition, the deficiency of cysteine (Cys) can lead to grey hair, skin lesion and slow body growth (Feng et al., 2020). Therefore, the development of effective and robust methods for thiols analysis appears to be particularly essential in the early diagnosis of certain diseases (Zhou et al., 2018). However, due to the highly similar molecular structures and chemical characteristics of thiols, the specific discrimination of thiols has become a great challenge (Li et al., 2019).

So far, a variety of methods have been explored to identify thiols, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Isokawa et al., 2013), fluorometry (Mandani et al., 2015), electrochemistry (Raoof et al., 2013), and so on. However, these methods often suffer from the disadvantages of expensive cost, complicated operations and deficient specificity, which thus hinder their further practical applications (Chen et al., 2018). Inspired by the human olfactory system, the array-based technology, also known as electronic tongue, has attracted much more attention in virtue of high throughput and superior accuracy (Sun et al., 2020). In general, the sensor array consists of a series of cross-reactive receptors that can interact with various target analytes to generate the unique “fingerprint” responses (Geng et al., 2019).

In particular, the machine learning algorithm has served as a powerful statistical analysis method, which can transform the complex multidimensional data generated by the high-throughput experiments to a simple visualized mode (Feng et al., 2020). For instance, the linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) enable the analysis of obtained “fingerprints” from sensor arrays for discrimination among different targets (Ma et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018).

In recent years, the nanozymes have intrigued tremendous research interest on account of their superior stability, low cost and tunable catalytic activities (Sang et al., 2020), which have been widely applied in the fields of biosensing, biomedicine, agriculture and environment (Huang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). In particular, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are small pieces of graphene with a size less than 100 nm in lateral dimension (Hai et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2022), which are explored as an emerging carbon-based peroxidase mimics (Hai et al. 2021a, 2021b; Sun et al., 2014). Enlightened by the inherent catalytic structure of natural enzymes, the catalytic activity of GQD nanozyme can be readily regulated (Huang et al., 2019). For example, it has been found that the ketonic carbonyl groups (-C=O) on GQDs can act as the catalytic activity site, while the intact aromatic structure, high surface area as well as the carboxylic groups (O=C–O-) can provide sufficient binding sites for substrates (Sun et al., 2015). However, the hydroxy groups (-C-OH) can inhibit the catalytic reaction (Sun et al., 2015). In addition, the metal ions, especially some transition metal ions, can serve as the cofactors or activating agents to enhance the catalytic activities of nanozymes (Nandu et al., 2020). Therefore, catalytic performance of GQDs can be readily regulated by various metal ions (Gao et al., 2007; Jana et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019), showing great potential in constructing sensor arrays.

Herein, we have constructed a cross-reactive colorimetric sensor array based on metal ion-integrated TPA@GQDs, achieving high discrimination of six thiols (Scheme 1 ). As a nanozyme with intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, the TPA@GQDs can catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which further oxidizes TMB to produce a green color with the maximum absorption wavelength at 650 nm. In this work, the sensing units are rationally designed by regulating the catalytic activities of TPA@GQDs using three transition metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) with disparate Fenton activities. The transition metal ions can coordinate with the abundant hydroxy groups on the surface of TPA@GQDs, inducing the formation of catalytic active centers. Consequently, the catalytical activities of each sensing unit (metal ion-TPA@GQD ensembles) is elevated to various degrees. Given the stronger binding affinity of metal ions with mercaptan than hydroxyl groups, the addition of thiols will preferentially combine with metal ions, resulting in the suppression of metal ion-upregulated catalytic properties of TPA@GQDs. Therefore, the diverse absorbance change in each sensing unit is generated accompanied by the unique colorimetric response to corresponding thiols. The resultant “fingerprint maps” can be processed by LDA for accurate discrimination of various types of thiols, a kind of thiol at different concentrations and the mixtures of thiols with various ratios. To assess the feasibility of this sensor array in practical applications, the discrimination of normal cells from cancerous ones is achieved according to the various levels of GSH in different types of cells, which offers potential evidence for accurate cancer diagnosis. Further, the patients with cardiovascular diseases have been identified based on the different content of Hcy in serum samples. Overall, this work proposed a simple, fast and high-throughput nanozyme-based sensor array for excellent discrimination and sensitive quantification of thiols, which holds great potential in disease identification and diagnosis in clinical applications.

Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2), copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), cysteine (Cys), dithiothreitol (DTT), and 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Glutathione (GSH), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) and terephthalic acid (TPA) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Homocysteine (Hcy) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Thioglycolic acid (TGA) was provided by Huixing Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were of analytical reagent grade. The deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) afforded for the preparation of solutions was supplied by Milli-Q ultrapure water system (Shanghai, China).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100F field-emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1.0 cm optical path. The fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained on a F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), which offered a 0.2 cm quartz cuvette. The FT-IR spectra were obtained by a Nicolet iS50 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an ESCALAB XI + analysis system (Thermo Fisher, USA).

The TPA@GQDs were prepared according to the previous report with minor modification (Hai et al., 2018). First, 0.10 g graphene oxide (GO) and 0.56 g KOH were ultrasonically mixed and dispersed in 10 mL of water. Then, 2 mL of GO suspension, 0.015 g TPA, 0.5 mL of H2O2 (30%) and 2.5 mL of H2O were added in sequence into a 25 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined stainless steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 5 h and cooled down to room temperature. The product was adjusted to neutral with HCl, followed by dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 2–3 days to obtain the pure TPA@GQDs solution. After rotary evaporation, the TPA@GQDs powder were quantified by lyophilization and redispersed in deionized water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of kinetics data of metal ion-TPA@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A 25 μL of TPA@GQDs (10 mg mL−1) and 5 μL of H2O2 (500 mM) were fully mixed in NaAc-HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.5) (the final concentrations of TPA@GQDs and H2O2 are 2.5 mg mL−1 and 25 mM, respectively). After that, 10 μL of TPA@GQDs-H2O2 solution and 69 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer were pipetted in a 96-well plate. Then, 10 μL of Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ (all at 5 mM) were added into the TPA@GQDs-H2O2 solution, respectively, followed by co-incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to form the sensing units (metal ion-TPA@GQD ensembles). Then, 10 μL of TMB with different concentrations (final concentration: 0–0.6 mM) was added to the above solution. Immediately, the UV–vis absorbance of TMB at 650 nm was monitored on a Synergy HTX multi-mode microplate reader (BioTek, USA) for 10 min. The Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum velocity (V max) values of the metal ion-TPA@GQDs were determined by fitting the Michaelis-Menten equation.

First, 10 μL of Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ (all at 5 mM) was added to the TPA@GQDs-H2O2 solution (10 μL, as depicted in 2.4) containing 69 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer, respectively, and co-incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to form the sensing units. Then, 10 μL of various target thiols were added into the above sensing unit solution and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 1 μL of TMB (50 mM, dissolved in alcohol) was added and incubated for another 30 min. Finally, the absorbance at 650 nm was recorded by microplate reader (Bio Tek, USA). By calculating the value of [(A-A0)/A0] (where A and A0 represent the absorbance of the sensing units containing TMB and H2O2 at 650 nm in the presence and absence of thiols, respectively), a “3 sensing units × 6 thiols × 6 replicates” training-data matrix was obtained, which was processed by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) in SPSS Statistics 17.0. The discrimination of a kind of thiol (Cys, GSH, Hcy, DTT, MCH and TGA) at different concentrations and the mixtures of two or three thiols with various ratios were also carried out according to the above-mentioned procedures. Further, to evaluate the anti-interference capability of this sensor array, the discrimination of thiols coexisting with real serum was performed according to the same procedures above.

To achieve the identification of cancers, the human normal liver cells (L02 cells), human breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells) and human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells) with various contents of GSH were discriminated using the proposed nanozyme-based sensor array. In brief, all the cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics. After washed with PBS buffer (10 mM) for three times, the cells were dispersed in DMEM medium and counted to prepare a cell suspension (1 × 106 cells mL−1). Subsequently, the cells were lysed using the ultrasonic method (5 s for each time with an interval of 10 s for 20 times). After centrifugation, the supernatant of cell lysate was diluted 10 times with DMEM, and incubated with the three metal ion-TPA@GQD sensing units containing H2O2 for 30 min. Subsequently, the TMB was added and incubated for another 30 min. Finally, the absorbance at 650 nm was recorded and the absorbance variation values [(A-A0)/A0] were calculated to obtained the training-data matrixes, which were further processed by LDA and HCA.

To identify the patients with cardiovascular disease from healthy volunteers, four serum samples with different concentrations of Hcy (8.62, 10.06, 19.25 and 33.47 μM) were discriminated using the proposed nanozyme-based sensor array. The 100 times-diluted serums were separately incubated with the three metal ion-TPA@GQD sensing units containing H2O2 for 30 min. After adding TMB into the system and incubating for another 30 min, the absorbance at 650 nm was recorded and the absorbance variation values of [(A-A0)/A0] were calculated to obtain the training-data matrixes, which were further processed by LDA and HCA.

All the serum samples were collected from the human blood provided by Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University (Qingdao, China), and all volunteers were received informed consent for our test in conformity to the Ethics Committee guidelines. All the experiments involving human samples and cells were conducted in accordance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, which was approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University.

In this study, the TPA@GQDs are readily prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method, in which the alkaline cutting process causes the structural defects in GQDs (Fig. 1 a). Meanwhile, the TPA can be perfectly conjugated onto the defect sites of GQDs through π-π interaction (Chen and Chen, 2015). The structure and chemical component of TPA@GQDs are studied by high-resolution C1s XPS analysis. The three deconvoluted peaks, located at 284.5 eV, 286 eV and 288.1 eV, are corresponding to C=C, C–O and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1b). In addition, the zeta potential of TPA@GQDs is measured to be −24.9 mV (Fig. 1c). The negative charge of TPA@GQDs can be attributed to the oxygen functional groups on the surface, which thus facilitate their combination with positively charged substrate, such as TMB and metal ions.

The optical properties of TPA@GQDs are investigated by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra, respectively. A favorable fluorescence behavior with a maximum excitation/emission wavelength at 430 nm/520 nm is observed (Fig. 1d), which shows a red shift compared with GQDs (430 nm/513 nm), suggesting the π-π conjugation effect in TPA@GQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of TPA@GQDs display a maximum absorption wavelength at 231 nm, which is attributed to the π-π transition on aromatic ring. In addition, the weak shoulder absorption at 261 nm is relevant to n-π transition of C=O. In comparison with GQDs, the absorption peak in TPA@GQDs shows a red shift owing to the formation of conjugation system (Fig. 1e). All the results indicate the successful preparation of TPA@GQDs, in which the TPA is functionalized on GQDs through π-π conjugation and multiple functional groups (e.g. hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups) are abundant in TPA@GQDs.

The peroxidase-like activity of TPA@GQD nanozyme is evaluated by the catalytic oxidation of substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2, in which the characteristic absorbance of the oxidized TMB at 650 nm is monitored. As shown in Fig. 1f and g, the TPA@GQDs display a high peroxidase-like catalytic activity, which can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate ·OH, resulting in the oxidization of TMB to produce an obvious green color with the maximum absorption wavelength at 650 nm. The favorable peroxidase-like catalytic activity of TPA@GQDs might be attributed to not only the intact aromatic structure with large surface area, but also the peripheral carbonyl/carboxylic moieties on the surface of GQDs. To confirm this hypothesis, the catalyzed oxidation of TMB by H2O2 using bare GQDs or TPA is also investigated (Fig. 1f and g). The GQD-catalyzed TMB-H2O2 system shows a slight increase in absorbance. Although TPA possesses the affluent carboxylic groups, it cannot catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2. After the modification of TPA on GQDs, both the aromatic structure of GQDs and carbonyl/carboxylic groups are gathered, which are synergistically contributed to the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of TPA@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study of the proposed sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the discrimination of different thiols, this sensor array is rationally designed based on TPA@GQD nanozyme using three transition metal ions as regulators (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+). The metal ions can coordinate with the abundant hydroxy groups on the surface of TPA@GQDs, leading to the formation of metal ion-regulated catalytic active centers (Fig. 2 a). On these grounds, the catalytic activity of metal ion-TPA@GQD ensembles can be modulated to various degrees, which is versatilely applied as the sensing units to construct the sensor array.

Further, the interaction between metal ions and TPA@GQDs is investigated by taking Cu2+-mediated sensing unit as an example. The TEM images of TPA@GQDs shows a good dispersibility with an average diameter size of 3.42 nm (Fig. 2b). In the presence of Cu2+, TPA@GQDs gradually gather to aggregate and Cu2+-TPA@GQD ensembles are formed due to the coordination of Cu2+ with the hydroxyl groups on TPA@GQDs, while each TPA@GQDs in the ensembles retains the structure of quasi-spherical sheet as pristine TPA@GQDs, suggesting that the metal ions cannot affect the geometry of TPA@GQD nanozyme (Fig. 2b). The FT-IR result further confirms the consumption of hydroxyl groups after the formation of Cu2+-TPA@GQD ensembles (Fig. 2c). Besides, the binding of another two metal ions (Fe2+ and Zn2+) at the catalytic center has also been investigated, reflecting the coordination ability of the metal ions with TPA@GQDs (Fig. S1). After introducing Cys to Cu2+-TPA@GQD ensembles, a dispersed state is presented again due to the stronger binding affinity of Cu2+ to Cys than hydroxyl groups (Fig. 2b), resulting in the disassembly of Cu2+-TPA@GQD ensembles. Accordingly, the content of hydroxyl groups is increased in Cu2+-TPA@GQDs after adding Cys (Fig. 2c), indicating the hydroxyl groups release triggered by thiols. Moreover, the S2p XPS spectra appear the peaks of thiolate (S2p 3/2, 161.8 eV) and disulfide (Cu–S, S2p 1/2, 163.1 eV) (Fig. 2d), also confirming the binding of Cu2+ (metal ions) with Cys (thiols) through coordination interaction.

Subsequently, the role and compatibility of metal ions on TPA@GQDs are investigated. Given that different metal ions have different ability to coordinate with hydroxyl groups, the catalytic activity of metal ion-TPA@GQDs can be enhanced to various degrees with the consumption of hydroxy groups. Moreover, it has been found that many transition metal ions can act as the Fenton or Fenton-like agents, which are able to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce ·OH (He et al., 2021). Thus, the metal ions can serve as the cofactor to enhance the catalytic activity of TPA@GQD nanozyme to various degree. The time-dependent absorbance kinetic curve of TMB-H2O2 at 650 nm catalyzed by various metal ion-TPA@GQDs (Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Au3+, Ag+, and Ca2+) has been explored (Fig. S2). The conversion rate of TMB to oxTMB is in accordance with the slope of the fitted line, which depends on the activity of metal ion-TPA@GQDs (Yan et al., 2018). Under the same concentration of metal ions and TPA@GQDs, the slope of TMB catalyzed by Fe2+-TPA@GQDs, Cu2+-TPA@GQDs and Zn2+-TPA@GQDs is 77.3-fold, 50.6-fold and 2.1-fold of TPA@GQDs, respectively, confirming the stimulatory effect for the three transition metal ions to the catalytical performance of TPA@GQDs. Whereas the slope of TMB catalyzed by Au3+-TPA@GQDs, Ag+-TPA@GQDs and Ca2+-TPA@GQDs are 4.5-fold, 0.9-fold and 0.6-fold of TPA@GQDs, indicating that Ag+ and Ca2+ hardly pose any positive contribution to the catalytic activity of TPA@GQDs. Although Au3+ shows similar catalytic boost as Zn2+, considering the cost, we choose Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ as the regulators for the construction of sensing units. In addition, the biochemical and kinetics data of each metal ion-regulated sensing units (metal ion-TPA@GQDs) have been investigated. The kinetics of metal ion-TPA@GQDs as mimic peroxidases are fitted with the typical Michaelis-Menten equations (Fig. S3), in which the kinetic parameters including Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum velocity (V max) are obtained (Table S1). The K m values of the three metal ion-TPA@GQDs (Fe2+-TPA@GQDs: 0.23 mM; Cu2+-TPA@GQDs: 0.24 mM; Zn2+-TPA@GQDs: 0.30 mM) are lower than that of TPA@GQDs (0.32 mM), suggesting that metal ions do not affect the affinity of TPA@GQDs to substrates, which further confirms the compatibility of the three metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) on TPA@GQDs. What's more, the V max of Fe2+-TPA@GQDs, Cu2+-TPA@GQDs and Zn2+-TPA@GQDs is higher than that of TPA@GQDs, respectively, which also validates the catalytic performance promotion of TPA@GQDs by the three metal ions.

Then, the metal ion-regulated catalytic activity improvement of TPA@GQDs and the inhibition effect of thiols are evaluated by monitoring the absorbance at 650 nm after catalyzed oxidation of TMB by H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2e, the catalytic activity of TPA@GQDs is enhanced significantly by Fe2+, a strong Fenton reagent (Gao et al., 2007; Jana et al., 2021). Since Cu2+ is a weak Fenton-like agent, a slight influence on the catalysis of TPA@GQDs is observed (Jana et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019), while Zn2+ is demonstrated to generated much less ·OH than Fe2+ or Cu2+, which hardly makes a difference on the catalytic effect of TPA@GQDs (Mendez-Garrido et al., 2014; Nandu et al., 2020). Further, the catalysis inhibition effect of thiols on metal ion-TPA@GQDs is investigated by taking Cys as an example. The Cys has induced various restrain degree on the catalytic performance of metal ion-TPA@GQD ensembles, which has hardly inhibition on either bare TPA@GQDs or single metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+). Specifically, since Fe2+ has the strong coordination capacity with Cys than Cu2+ and Zn2+ (Haas and Franz, 2009), the catalytic ability of Fe2+-TPA@GQDs is most severely suppressed by Cys. In Cu2+-mediated sensing units, although the reduction of Cu2+ by sulfhydryl groups can produce Cu+ with greater Fenton-like activity (the Cu 2p XPS spectra in Fig. 2d) (Fu et al., 2021), the catalysis inhibition effect is still exerted given the relatively low thiol concentration (300 μM). As for the Zn2+-TPA@GQDs sensing unit, only a little inhibition is observed on the catalytic activity. It might be reasonable to conclude that the catalytic activity of each sensing unit will be altered to various degree with great distinction upon treated with different thiols.

Machine learning algorithm is an important means for thiols discrimination, which can analyze and obtain rules (models) from data, and then make predictions for unknown data, achieving the analysis of complex samples (Shen et al., 2022). To evaluate the discrimination performance of the proposed nanozyme sensor array, six thiols, including Cys, GSH, Hcy, DTT, MCH and TGA, are adopted as the target analytes (Table S2). Six parallels are arranged for each thiol detection to avoid the contingency and ensure the accuracy. The different binding affinities between thiols and metal ions result in the diverse catalysis restraint degrees of each metal ion-TPA@GQD sensing unit. Through using TMB as the substrate, the unique absorbance changes of [(A-A0)/A0] with colorimetric response patterns are discerned in the presence of H2O2, where A and A0 represent the absorbance of metal ion-TPA@GQDs in the presence and absence of thiols (300 μM), respectively (Fig. 3 a and Table S3). Accordingly, the heat map in response to different kinds of thiols, also acted as the “fingerprints”, is generated, revealing the feasibility of this nanozyme-based sensor array in the discrimination of six thiols at 300 μM (Fig. 3b). Then, the colorimetric response patterns (3 sensing units × 6 targets × 6 replicates) are subjected to LDA using the first two most important discrimination factors to transform the complicated training-data matrixes into visual 2D canonical score plots (Fig. 3c). The LDA results display that the same kind of thiols are united to a cluster, while the different types of thiols can be completely separated without errors or misclassifications. As the control, the identification of six thiols at 300 μM using only metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) as the sensing units is also evaluated (Fig. S4 and Table S4). In this case, the parallelism between the same kind of thiols is poor, and the overlap between different types of thiols is serious, indicating that the single metal ions cannot be used as the sensing units for thiol discrimination. The excellent discrimination ability of metal ion-TPA@GQDs toward thiols can be attributed to the use of TPA@GQDs as the basic materials. On one hand, the exposure of hydroxyl groups in TPA@GQDs induced by the chelation between thiols and metal ions can improve the response of each sensing unit to thiol. On the other hand, the negatively charged TPA@GQDs with conjugated structure are more likely to combine with positively charged TMB substrate and metal ion regulators (Liu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017), making the catalytic reaction occurs at the interface with increased reaction rate and degree (Sun et al., 2015). It is worth noting that in the Fe2+-mediated sensing unit, nitrate ions (NO3 -) exhibit oxidizability under the acidic reaction system, which can oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+. Thus, ferrous chloride (FeCl2) is chosen rather than ferrous nitrate (Fe(NO3)2). As for the Cu2+- and Zn2+-mediated sensing units, anions (NO3 - or Cl−) of the metal salts does not affect the performance of the sensor array (Fig. S5, Table S5). Considering the cost of the reagents, we choose FeCl2, Cu(NO3)2 and Zn(NO3)2 as the regulators. To investigate the detection performance, the six thiols at different concentrations are analyzed using the proposed sensor array. When the concentration of thiols is higher than 50 nM (300 μM, 100 μM, 1 μM, 100 nM, and 50 nM), the six thiols can be perfectly distinguished from each other with a 100% accuracy (Fig. 3d–g and Tables S6–S9), indicating the excellent identification capability in a wide range of concentrations. However, when the concentration of thiols is below 25 nM, the overlaps and misidentification are occurred between different thiols (Fig. 3h and Table S10). Therefore, this sensor array provides a sensitive identification of thiols as low as 50 nM, which is much lower than the thiols in serum, paving a robust way for the discrimination of real samples.

To further validate the quantitative capability of this nanozyme sensor array, the discrimination assay of one thiol with various concentrations is explored by taking Cys as an example. The absorbance changes of sensor array toward different concentrations of Cys are recorded by the heat map (Fig. 4 a and Table S11). As shown in Fig. 4b, When the concentration of Cys is ranging from 1 μM to 300 μM, it can be well discriminated from the 2D canonical score plot without overlapping with each other. Besides, the 2D canonical score plot also indicates that the factor 1 (99.3%) is greater than 60%, which is acceptably employed to correlate the concentrations of Cys (Feng et al., 2020). As the increase of Cys concentration, the value of factor 1 is decreased. A good linear relationship is obtained between score of factor 1 versus the concentration of Cys ranging from 1 to 300 μM (Fig. 4c). Similarly, the quantification of other thiols has also achieved with a linear range of 1–50 μM for GSH (Fig. S6 and Table S12), 0.1–1.5 μM for Hcy (Fig. S7 and Table S13), 1–10 μM for DTT (Fig. S8 and Table S14), 1–50 μM for MCH (Fig. S9 and Table S15), 1–9 μM for TGA (Fig. S10 and Table S16), respectively. These results demonstrate the high sensitivity and wide linear range of this nanozyme-based sensor array for thiols determination.

To determinate the multiple differentiation ability of the sensor array, the coexisting thiols is analyzed. The binary thiol mixtures (Cys: GSH = 0 : 100%, 25%: 75%, 50%: 50%, 75%: 25% and 100%: 0, GSH: Hcy = 0 : 100%, 25%: 75%, 50%: 50%, 75%: 25% and 100%: 0, a total concentration of 100 μM) and ternary thiol mixtures (Cys: GSH: Hcy = 10%: 10%: 80%, 20%: 20%: 60% and 30%: 30%: 40%, at 100 μM; 100% of Cys, GSH and Hcy, a total concentration of 100 μM) are selected as the target analytes. As shown in Fig. 4d–f, the thiols in the mixtures with different molar ratios can be well aggregated into different groups, achieving a 100% accuracy to separate and discriminate from each other (Tables S17–S19), which lays a foundation for the identification and differentiation of complicated real samples.

To assess the practicability of the sensor array, the blind samples test and interference experiments are implemented. Notably, all the 21 blind samples can be classified accurately (Table S20). Further, the feasibility of thiol discrimination in the presence of interferents is conducted. According to the literature (Feng et al., 2020), the human serum containing various electrolytes, phosphates, proteins, and carboxylate ions can be utilized as the interfering substances to perform the interference experiments. In our work, the six thiols in human serums can be identified precisely in terms of the 2D canonical score plot (Fig. S11 and Table S21). Besides, the mixture of sulfur compounds including sulfur dioxide, hydrogen sulfide and polysulfide (e.g. sodium thiosulfate) also have no influence on the discrimination of thiols (Fig. S12, Table S22). Therefore, the sensor array displays a satisfactory anti-interference ability in complex samples, which gives evidence to demonstrate its viability in real applications.

To assess the feasibility of the nanozyme-based sensor array in practical applications, the discrimination of various types of cells or clinical human serums has been carried out based on the distinct levels of thiols in various samples using LDA and HCA, achieving the versatile identification of diseases (Fig. 5 a). In general, there is a close relationship between some diseases and thiol levels in human bodies. For example, it has been reported that GSH is a significant cancer biomarker that is much higher in cancerous than in normal cells. Thus, the monitoring of GSH content in actual cell samples is an effective way for diagnosis of cancer. (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Herein, the concentration of GSH in human normal liver cells (L02), human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) and human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) is respectively detected to verify the feasibility of this sensor array for cell identification. The heat map against the normal and cancerous cells is acquired (Fig. S13), and the training-data is subjected to the LDA. As expected, the three types of cells can be precisely classified without misclassification (Fig. 5b and Table S23). Based on the distinct difference in GSH concentrations, the corresponding clusters of cancerous cells (HeLa and MCF-7) with high concentration of GSH are located at the left area in the 2D canonical score plot, which are far from the cluster of normal cells (L02) on the right region. Besides, the violin chart displays a significant difference in the score of discriminant factor 1 between normal and cancerous cells (Fig. 5c). The HCA is processed to reflect the classification capacity of the sensor array more intuitively (Fig. 5d). The raw training-data are subjected to HCA based on Euclidean distance (Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2016). It is obvious that all the three kinds of cells can be accurately distributed in their respective groups without misclassification. Besides, the cancerous HeLa and MCF-7 cells are further assigned to the same cluster since they have a similar high GSH concentration compared with the normal L02 cells. To further assess the performance of LDA for disease identification, the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve is obtained by plotting the sensitivity (true-positive rate, TRP) on the ordinate and 1-specificity (false-positive rate, FPR) on the abscissa (Fig. 5e) (Carter et al., 2016). In general, the area under the ROC curve (AUC) reflects the performance of a diagnostic test, the closer the AUC is to 1, the better the diagnostic effect is. Besides, an ideal diagnostic test has a TPR of 1, an FPR of 0 (Liu et al., 2021). In this cell classification model, the AUC, TPR and FPR is 1, 1 and 0, respectively, which demonstrate that the sensor array can be applied in discrimination of cancer versus health with high accuracy (Fig. 5e and Table S24).

It has been reported that the blood Hcy in healthy volunteers ranges from 5 to 15 μM, while it can be diagnosed as the hyperhomocysteinemia when the concentration of Hcy is higher than 15 μM. Thus, Hcy can be considered as an independent risk factor for the cardiovascular disease (Azad et al., 2018). In this study, four serum samples with various concentrations of Hcy are selected as the analytes, in which two samples are collected from the patients suffered from cardiovascular disease (the measured Hcy concentrations are 19.25 μM and 33.47 μM, respectively), and the other two ones are from the healthy volunteers (the measured Hcy concentrations are 8.62 μM and 10.06 μM, respectively). The heap map of the sensor array against the four serum samples are attained (Fig. S14), which are further transformed to 2D canonical score plots by LDA model (Fig. 5f). Obviously, the two serum samples with the concentration of Hcy higher than 15 μM are classified on the left region of the canonical score plot, while the other two samples with the concentration of Hcy lower than 15 μM are on the right without overlapping parts. The discrimination presents a 100% accuracy (Table S25), which thus demonstrates a potential ability of the proposed sensor array in identification of cardiovascular disease. In addition, the violin chart elaborates the significant difference in score of discriminant factor 1 between healthy volunteers and cardiovascular disease (Fig. 5g). In the HCA plot (Fig. 5h), the healthy and diseased samples are clearly divided into two clusters, demonstrating the excellent identification ability of our sensor array in cardiovascular disease according to the distinct Hcy concentration in serum samples. Furthermore, based on the information provided by the ROC curve (AUC = 1, TPR = 1, FPR = 0), the sensor array demonstrates an excellent performance in the diagnosis of cardiovascular disease (Fig. 5i and Table S26). Overall, the as-constructed TPA@GQD-based nanozyme sensor array is expected to be a powerful tool in assisting the diseases identification and diagnosis in clinical applications.

In summary, a metal ion-TPA@GQD nanozyme-based colorimetric sensor array is fabricated, which is successfully applied for the discrimination of six thiols (Cys, GSH, Hcy, DTT, MCH and TGA). In this study, the sufficient substrate binding sites in TPA@GQDs synergistically promote the catalytic ability of sensing units, and three metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) is adopted to regulate the catalytic active centers. Owning to the different binding affinities between metal ions and thiols, the catalytic ability of the sensing units can be restrained to various degrees, leading to a unique colorimetric response for each thiol. By using the machine learning method like LDA, our work has provided a feasible solution for discrimination of thiols with various types and concentrations, even the mixture of thiols at different ratios with the accuracies of 100%. Remarkably, this nanozyme sensor array has been readily applied in identification of thiol-associated diseases (e.g. cancer or cardiovascular disease). Overall, the proposed nanozyme-based sensor array has demonstrated the advantages of simplicity, low cost and high throughput, which holds great potential in medical diagnosis.

Xueying Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tian Li: Investigation, Validation. Xin Hai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Sai Bi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004077 and 22076087), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671988), the Special Funds of the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqn20161028), the Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2020JQ08), and the Youth Innovation Technology Program of Shandong Province (2019KJC029).
